title: Alexander the Great
eyebrow: The boy who conquered the world
lede: In a reign of barely a dozen years, a young Macedonian king led his army from Greece to the edge of India, toppling the mightiest empire on Earth and dying undefeated at thirty-two. Alexander conquered an empire that did not outlive him — but spread Greek culture across three continents in a way that did.

maps.caption: From Macedon through Asia Minor, Egypt and Persia to the Indus — the largest empire the ancient world had yet seen, built in a decade.

images.title: Bust of Alexander

videos.title: The unbeaten general

body.text: A Prince Schooled by Aristotle

Alexander was born in 356 BCE in **Macedon**, a half-Greek kingdom on the northern fringe of the Greek world that the southern cities regarded as rough and backward. His father, **Philip II**, was a brilliant soldier-king who reformed the Macedonian army into the deadliest fighting force in Greece and brought the quarrelling city-states under his control. To educate his gifted son, Philip hired the finest mind of the age — the philosopher **Aristotle**, himself a student of Plato.

For three years the boy was tutored in [philosophy](/article/greek-philosophy), science, medicine, and the poetry of Homer. Alexander is said to have slept with a copy of the *Iliad*, the epic of the [Trojan War](/article/trojan-war), beneath his pillow, modelling himself on its hero Achilles. When Philip was assassinated in 336 BCE, the twenty-year-old inherited a battle-hardened army and an unfinished ambition: to lead the Greeks in revenge against the Persian Empire that had once invaded their homeland.

body.title: A perfect weapon, ready to hand
body.text: Alexander's genius was real, but he also inherited a finished instrument. Philip had already forged the Macedonian phalanx — bristling with eighteen-foot pikes — and the elite cavalry that Alexander would hurl at the enemy's centre. The son took up a sword his father had sharpened. ## The Unbeaten Decade In 334 BCE Alexander crossed into Asia and never lost a major battle. At the **Granicus**, at **Issus**, and finally at **Gaugamela** in 331 BCE, he shattered far larger Persian armies, twice putting the Great King **Darius III** to flight in person. Between these victories he marched down the Mediterranean coast, and in **Egypt** he was welcomed as a liberator, hailed as a pharaoh and a son of the god Amun, and founded the city of **Alexandria** that would become the intellectual capital of the ancient world. With the Persian heartland in his hands, Alexander could have stopped. He did not. Driven by a longing the Greeks called *pothos*, he pressed on east — over the mountains of Afghanistan, across the rivers of Central Asia, and into **India**, where his exhausted men at last refused to go further. Reluctantly he turned back, leading part of his army on a terrible march through the desert that killed thousands. > I am dying from the treatment of too many physicians. The bitter remark attributed to him on his deathbed catches the strangeness of his end. In 323 BCE, back in **Babylon** and planning still greater campaigns, Alexander fell ill — perhaps from fever, perhaps from drink, perhaps from poison — and died at thirty-two. Asked to whom he left his empire, he is said to have answered only, "To the strongest." ## The Empire That Tore Itself Apart There was no strongest. Alexander left no capable heir, and his generals — the **Diadochi**, or "Successors" — fell to fighting over the spoils. Within a generation his vast empire had splintered into rival kingdoms: the **Ptolemies** in [Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt), the **Seleucids** across Asia, the **Antigonids** in Macedon. The political achievement collapsed almost as fast as it had been built. :::note Conquest as cultural fusion Alexander founded dozens of cities, settled Greek soldiers across Asia, and even encouraged his men to marry Persian wives — taking a Persian bride himself. Whether from policy or vision, he set in motion a mingling of Greek and Eastern worlds that long outlasted his throne. ## A Longer Kind of Victory What survived was not the empire but its culture. In the kingdoms of the Successors, Greek became the language of government, trade, and learning from the Nile to the Hindu Kush. This was the [Hellenistic Age](/article/hellenistic-age) — a world in which Greek art, science, and ideas fused with those of Egypt, Persia, and beyond, and reached more people than the classical city-states ever had. Centuries later, [Rome](/article/roman-empire) would inherit that Greek-speaking eastern world wholesale, and ambitious Romans from [Julius Caesar](/article/julius-caesar) onward measured themselves against Alexander's legend. He became the eternal model of the conqueror: the young king who wept, the story goes, because there were no more worlds to conquer — and who, by dying so soon, never had to grow old or be defeated.

title: Ancient Architecture
lede: From the first stone columns to soaring temple halls, the ancient world learned to shape space into meaning — and the techniques born along the Nile, the Tigris, and the Aegean still underpin how we build today.

body.text: Architecture as Meaning

Before architecture was engineering it was **theology**. The earliest monumental buildings — Egyptian temples and tombs, Mesopotamian ziggurats — were designed to house gods, stage rituals, and model the cosmos in stone. Form followed *belief* as much as function: a temple was a map of creation, its dark inner sanctuary the mound that first rose from the waters.
The Egyptian Contribution
Egypt gave the world some of the foundational moves of architecture:

items: **Stone columns** — Egyptian builders carved columns shaped like bundled papyrus and lotus stems, among the earliest monumental colonnades anywhere.
**The hypostyle hall** — vast roofed spaces filled with columns, as at **Karnak**, whose Great Hypostyle Hall packs 134 columns into a single chamber.
**Axial, processional design** — temples laid out along a sacred axis, leading the visitor from open courts through ever-darker, ever-holier inner rooms.
**Monumental masonry** — the quarrying, moving, and precise placing of enormous stone blocks, perfected on the [Pyramids of Giza](/article/pyramids-of-giza).

body.title: Post and lintel
body.text: Most ancient architecture rests on the **post-and-lintel** system: vertical posts supporting horizontal beams. It is simple and strong in compression but limits how wide a space can be spanned — which is why ancient halls are forests of closely spaced columns. The Roman **arch** and **vault** would later break that limit.
A Sky-Aligned Craft

Ancient builders routinely oriented their monuments to the heavens — to the cardinal points, to the solstice sunrise, or to the rising of significant stars. Egyptian temples and pyramids were set out with the help of stellar observation, binding architecture to [astronomy](/article/egyptian-astronomy) and to the calendar that governed religious life.
A great building was never only shelter. It was an argument — about the gods, the king, and the order of the world — written in stone so that it could not be forgotten.

These ideas of proportion, procession, and permanence flowed outward to Greece and Rome and, ultimately, into the entire Western architectural tradition — a lineage you can keep following through the wider [architecture](/category/architecture) category.

title: Ancient Egypt
lede: For three thousand years, a thin green ribbon of life along the Nile sustained one of the most enduring civilizations the world has known — a society of god-kings, monumental stone, and a calm certainty about the order of the cosmos.

body.text: The Gift of the Nile

The Greek historian Herodotus called Egypt "the gift of the Nile," and the phrase has never been bettered. Every summer, monsoon rains far to the south swelled the river until it overflowed its banks across the valley floor, laying down a fresh sheet of black, fertile silt. When the waters withdrew, farmers planted into the renewed earth. This **annual inundation** was so dependable that the Egyptians built their entire calendar, economy, and worldview around it.

The contrast was stark and visible. The Egyptians called their narrow strip of cultivated land **Kemet**, the "Black Land," and the lifeless desert beyond it **Deshret**, the "Red Land." Between flood and drought, life and death, order and chaos, ran a boundary you could step across in an afternoon. That daily experience of a sharp, life-giving order shaped everything that followed.

body.title: The geography *was* the ideology
body.text: Because the Nile flowed north while the prevailing wind blew south, boats could drift downstream and sail upstream with ease. A single river bound the whole country into one trade and communication network — which is a large part of why Egypt could be unified, and stay unified, for so long.
One Land, Many Ages

Around **3100 BCE**, tradition holds that a southern king — remembered as Narmer or Menes — unified the kingdoms of Upper Egypt (the southern valley) and Lower Egypt (the northern delta) into a single state. Historians since have organised the roughly three thousand years that followed into thirty-odd royal **dynasties**, grouped into a few great ages of strength separated by "Intermediate Periods" of fragmentation:

items: **Old Kingdom** (c. 2686–2181 BCE) — the age of the great pyramid-builders at Giza.
**Middle Kingdom** (c. 2055–1650 BCE) — a literary and administrative golden age.
**New Kingdom** (c. 1550–1069 BCE) — empire, wealth, and famous rulers from Hatshepsut to Ramesses II.

body.text: What is astonishing is the continuity. A child in the New Kingdom looked back on the pyramids as we look back on the Roman Empire — already impossibly ancient. The same gods, the same script, and the same essential vision of the world persisted across that vast span.
Religion and the Cosmos

To the Egyptians, the universe ran on **maat** — truth, balance, and cosmic order — which it was the sacred duty of gods and kings to uphold against **isfet**, chaos. Their pantheon was vast and fluid: **Ra** the sun, sailing his barque across the sky each day and through the underworld each night; **Osiris**, lord of the dead and of resurrection; **Isis**, his wife, the great mother and magician; **Horus**, their falcon-headed son, embodied in the living king; and **Anubis**, who guided souls and weighed the heart.
They believed that in the hall of judgement, the heart of the dead was weighed against the feather of Maat. A life lived in balance passed on to the Field of Reeds; a heavy heart was devoured.

This was not a religion of dread so much as one of **continuity** — the conviction that death was a passage, not an ending, and that the right rituals, tombs, and texts could carry a person safely through it. (Follow the journey rail to explore [Egyptian religion](/article/egyptian-religion) in depth.)
The Pharaoh: God on Earth

At the centre of this order stood the **pharaoh** — not merely a ruler but a living link between the human and divine. He was Horus while he reigned and became Osiris when he died. His titles, his crowns, and the colossal scale of his monuments all proclaimed a single message: that the king guaranteed maat, and through him the sun rose, the river flooded, and the harvest came.

In practice the king governed through an immense bureaucracy of scribes, priests, and officials — the **vizier** chief among them — who recorded harvests, collected taxes in grain, organised labour, and dispensed justice in his name. Egypt was, in a real sense, the world's first great administrative state.
Monuments in Stone

No civilization has matched the Egyptians' instinct for building in a way that defied time. The **Great Pyramid of Khufu**, raised around 2560 BCE, stood as the tallest human-made structure on Earth for nearly four thousand years. It was built from millions of limestone blocks with breathtaking precision — its base is level to within a few centimetres and its sides aligned almost exactly to the cardinal directions.

Later kings turned to vast temple complexes — **Karnak**, **Luxor**, **Abu Simbel** — and to the hidden rock-cut tombs of the **Valley of the Kings**, hoping to foil the robbers who had plundered the conspicuous pyramids. The architecture was sacred engineering: every column, axis, and orientation carried meaning. (The journey continues through the [Pyramids of Giza](/article/pyramids-of-giza) and on to [ancient architecture](/article/ancient-architecture).)
Writing, Science, and the Stars

The Egyptians developed **hieroglyphs** — a script of several hundred signs, part picture and part sound — around the dawn of the dynastic age. For everyday accounts and letters they used a faster cursive form, **hieratic**, written in ink on **papyrus**, the paper-like material made from a river reed that gave us our very word for "paper."
Their practical knowledge was formidable:

items: **Astronomy** — they tracked the stars to fix their calendar, timing the new year to the dawn rising of **Sirius**, which heralded the flood.
**Mathematics** — they used geometry to re-survey fields erased by the inundation and to lay out monuments.
**Medicine** — surviving papyri describe diagnoses, treatments, and surgery with surprising empirical care.
**Engineering** — quarrying, moving, and raising stones of colossal weight without iron tools or the wheel-and-pulley as we know them.

body.text: Daily Life Along the River

For all the gods and god-kings, most Egyptians were farmers who lived close to the river, growing emmer wheat and barley, brewing beer, and baking bread — the two staples that also served as wages. Families were tightly knit; women held legal rights to own property, divorce, and conduct business that were remarkable for the ancient world. People loved their gardens, their pets (cats especially were cherished), board games like **senet**, music, and festivals that filled the religious calendar.

body.title: A culture obsessed with life
body.text: It is a common misreading to think the Egyptians were preoccupied with death. Their elaborate tombs and **mummification** were, in fact, an expression of how deeply they *loved* life — and how confident they were of continuing it.
Decline, Conquest, and Legacy

No order lasts forever, even one built to. After the New Kingdom, Egypt fell increasingly under foreign sway — Libyan, Nubian, Assyrian, and Persian rulers in turn. In **332 BCE** Alexander the Great took Egypt without a fight, and his general Ptolemy founded a Greek-speaking dynasty that ended with the most famous of all Egyptian rulers, **Cleopatra VII**. With her death in **30 BCE**, Egypt became a province of Rome.

Yet the civilization never truly vanished. Its monuments still stand; its art still astonishes; and when the **Rosetta Stone** allowed Jean-François Champollion to decipher hieroglyphs in **1822**, three thousand years of silent stone began to speak again. From the calendar to the column, from monumental architecture to the very idea of a unified state, ancient Egypt remains woven into the foundations of the modern world — a thread you can still follow, all the way to [Egypt today](/article/modern-egypt).

title: Ancient Greece
eyebrow: The seedbed of the Western mind
lede: Scattered across a thousand rocky islands and tangled peninsulas, the Greeks never built a single empire — yet from their quarrelsome little city-states came democracy, philosophy, theatre, the Olympic ideal, and a way of asking questions about the world that the West has never stopped using.

body.text: A Civilization Without a Capital

Ancient Greece was never a country. There was no single Greek state, no capital, no king of all the Greeks. Instead there were hundreds of small, fiercely independent cities, each clinging to its own valley or island, each with its own laws, gods, coins, and dialect. What bound them together was not a government but a shared idea of themselves: they spoke the same language, worshipped the same Olympian gods, read the same poems of **Homer**, and gathered every four years at Olympia to compete as Greeks against Greeks.

The geography made it so. Mainland Greece is a crumpled land of mountains and narrow plains, its coastline broken into a thousand harbours and fringed by islands scattered across the Aegean like stepping-stones. A people penned into such small, separate pockets of farmland naturally formed small, separate communities. The Greek word for such a community was the **polis** — usually translated "city-state," though it meant something closer to the whole body of citizens bound together by custom and law.

body.title: The polis was the unit of Greek life
body.text: A polis was not just a place but a community of citizens who governed themselves. Out of the intense, face-to-face politics of these small cities — where every citizen could know the issues and many could speak — came the radical idea that ordinary people, not kings or priests, should rule themselves. ## From Dark Age to Golden Age The Greek story did not begin in glory. An earlier civilization, the **Mycenaeans** of Agamemnon's legendary age, collapsed around 1200 BCE, and Greece sank into centuries so poor and illiterate that historians call them a Dark Age. Out of that darkness, slowly, the Greeks rebuilt — and around 800 BCE they borrowed an alphabet from the Phoenicians, founded colonies from Spain to the Black Sea, and began composing the poetry and politics that would change the world. The high point came in the fifth century BCE, the **Classical Age**. Against all odds the Greek cities, led by Athens and Sparta, threw back two massive invasions by the Persian Empire. The confidence of that victory poured into a single extraordinary generation in Athens. In the span of a lifetime its citizens built the Parthenon, invented the theatre of tragedy and comedy, practised the world's first [democracy](/article/greek-democracy), and — in the questions of **Socrates** — turned [philosophy](/article/greek-philosophy) toward the examined human life. > Man is the measure of all things. The sophist Protagoras caught the spirit of the age in that famous phrase. The Greeks placed the human being, with all its reason and folly, at the centre of their art, their politics, and their thought — a turn of mind that the West has inherited ever since. ## Athens and Sparta Two cities came to embody the poles of the Greek character. **Athens** was a democracy, a sea power, a place of restless talk, trade, art, and argument — open, brilliant, and unstable. **Sparta** was its opposite: a grim militarized state where boys were taken from their families to be raised as soldiers and the highest virtue was discipline in the service of the city. The Greeks themselves debated endlessly which model was better, and their rivalry erupted into the long, ruinous **Peloponnesian War** that exhausted both. :::note A glory built on contradiction The same Athens that invented democracy denied all political rights to women, resident foreigners, and a vast population of slaves. Greek freedom was real and revolutionary — and it was also partial, resting on the labour of the unfree. To study Greece honestly is to hold both truths at once. ## The Greek Inheritance For all their quarrels, the Greeks left a legacy out of all proportion to their numbers. They were swallowed first by the empire of [Alexander the Great](/article/alexander-the-great), a Macedonian who carried Greek language and culture as far as India, and then by [Rome](/article/rome), which conquered Greece militarily but was itself conquered culturally — taking Greek gods, Greek art, and Greek learning as its own. Through Rome and later through the Renaissance, the Greek inheritance flowed into the foundations of Europe. When we vote in an assembly, watch a drama, reason from evidence, admire a columned building, or compete in the Olympic Games, we are speaking a language first spoken on these rocky shores. Ancient Greece built no lasting empire of its own — and yet, through ideas alone, it conquered the imagination of the world. To trace its threads outward is to follow them into [Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt), into Rome, and into nearly every corner of the West that came after.

title: Ancient Rome
lede: From a cluster of huts on seven hills above the Tiber, Rome grew into a civilization that ruled the Mediterranean world for a thousand years — and whose law, language, roads, and idea of the city still run beneath the modern West like buried foundations.

body.text: A City Born of the Tiber

Rome began where geography invited it. A bend in the river Tiber, fordable yet defensible, lay a short way inland from the sea — close enough to trade, far enough to be safe from pirates. Around it rose a ring of low hills whose villages, over generations, grew together into a single town. Tradition fixed the founding in **753 BCE** and gave it to **Romulus**, who according to legend killed his twin **Remus** and named the city for himself. The truth was slower and less dramatic: Rome was not founded so much as it accumulated, a settlement of Latins and Sabines that learned early to absorb its neighbours rather than merely conquer them.

That instinct for absorption became Rome's genius. Where other ancient cities guarded citizenship jealously, Rome extended it — first to defeated Latin towns, later to whole provinces — until a man born in Spain, Africa, or Syria could call himself Roman and even rule as emperor. A city that might have remained one town among many instead became a machine for turning outsiders into citizens.

body.title: Rome's secret weapon was citizenship
body.text: Most ancient empires ruled subjects. Rome, uniquely, manufactured Romans. By steadily widening who counted as one of its own, it bound conquered peoples to its fortunes — and gave them a stake in defending it. ## Three Constitutions, One Name The thousand-year arc of Rome falls into three great political ages. It began as a **monarchy**, ruled by kings — some Latin, some Etruscan — until, tradition says, the Romans expelled the tyrant Tarquin in **509 BCE** and swore never again to bow to a king. What replaced him was the [Roman Republic](/article/roman-republic): a layered system of elected magistrates, a powerful Senate, and popular assemblies, designed so that no single man could seize total power. For nearly five centuries the Republic carried Rome from a regional power to the master of the Mediterranean. But the very conquests that made it great also broke it. Generals grew richer and more loyal to their armies than to the state, and a century of civil wars ended only when one man, the adopted heir of [Julius Caesar](/article/julius-caesar), gathered every office into his own hands. As **Augustus**, he founded the [Roman Empire](/article/roman-empire) in **27 BCE** — a monarchy in all but name, which Romans accepted because it brought peace. ## What Rome Built Roman power rested on more than [the legions](/article/roman-army). It rested on organisation made visible in stone. Roads ran arrow-straight across whole provinces; aqueducts carried mountain water into city fountains; concrete vaults spanned spaces no Greek architect had dared. The achievements of [Roman engineering](/article/roman-engineering) were not decoration but infrastructure — the plumbing of empire, built to move armies, grain, and water with relentless efficiency. Above all, Rome built **law**. The idea that authority should rest on written, public rules rather than the whim of a ruler; that a person was innocent until proven guilty; that contracts and property had standing a magistrate must respect — these Roman habits of mind passed into the legal systems of Europe and, through them, much of the world. > They make a desert and call it peace. The historian Tacitus put those bitter words in the mouth of a conquered chieftain, and they capture the cost of Rome honestly. Empire was built on conquest, slavery, and the crushing of those who resisted. Yet within its borders Rome also built something durable: two centuries of relative peace, the **Pax Romana**, in which trade, cities, and ideas flowed freely from Britain to the Euphrates. ## A Mediterranean World Rome did not rule in isolation. Its world was the whole Mediterranean basin, and it drank deeply from the cultures it absorbed. It took its gods and much of its art from Greece; it fed its people on Egyptian grain and was bewitched by Egyptian style, weaving the worship of **Isis** into its own [religion](/article/roman-religion). When Caesar sailed to [Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt) and met Cleopatra, two ancient worlds met at the height of their fame. :::note An empire that never quite ended When the western emperors fell in 476 CE, Rome's eastern half lived on for another thousand years as Byzantium, and its Church, law, and Latin tongue shaped everything after. Rome did not so much die as dissolve into the foundations of the world that followed — a story the [fall of Rome](/article/fall-of-rome) tells in full. To study Rome is to study how a community organises power, defends itself, and decides who belongs — the oldest questions of political life, asked at the scale of a continent and answered, for a thousand years, in Latin.

title: Black Holes
lede: A black hole is a place where gravity has become so overwhelming that nothing — not even light — can escape. Once the stuff of pure theory, these dark engines have now been heard as ripples in space, photographed as rings of fire, and found lurking at the heart of nearly every galaxy.

maps.title: Anatomy of a black hole
maps.caption: The singularity, the event horizon, the photon ring and the swirling accretion disk that betrays a black hole's presence.

images.caption: The supermassive black hole at the heart of galaxy M87, ringed by glowing gas — released by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019.

images.title: Gravitational waves
images.caption: A simulation of two black holes spiralling together — collisions that shake the fabric of spacetime itself.

videos.title: What falls into a black hole?

body.text: The Point of No Return

To understand a black hole, start with **escape velocity** — the speed something must reach to break free of a body's gravity. From Earth it is about 11 kilometres per second; from a more massive world it is higher. Now imagine packing so much mass into so small a space that the escape velocity climbs all the way to the speed of light. At that point, nothing can leave. Not a rocket, not a particle, not even light itself. That object is a black hole.

The boundary around it is called the **event horizon** — not a physical surface, but a line in space marking the point of no return. Cross it, and every possible path leads inward. From the outside, the horizon is perfectly dark, because no light can climb back out to reach your eye. This is why a black hole is black.

body.title: Black holes are not cosmic vacuum cleaners
body.text: A common myth is that black holes roam space sucking everything in. In truth, their gravity at a distance is no stronger than any other object of the same mass. If the Sun were replaced by a black hole of identical mass, Earth would keep orbiting exactly as it does now — it would simply go dark and cold. You only fall in if you get very close.
Predicted by Einstein, Found in the Sky

Black holes fell out of **Einstein's general theory of relativity**, which in 1915 reimagined gravity not as a force but as the bending of spacetime by mass. Within months, the physicist **Karl Schwarzschild** solved Einstein's equations and found they allowed for a region from which nothing could escape. For decades most scientists, Einstein included, doubted such things could really exist.

They were wrong. Over the twentieth century, astronomers found stars whirling around invisible companions, and gas blazing in X-rays as it spiralled toward something unseen. The evidence mounted until the existence of black holes became impossible to deny — and the once-fanciful name, popularised by the physicist **John Wheeler** in the 1960s, entered the language for good.
How Black Holes Are Born
Black holes come in a range of sizes, formed in different ways.

items: **Stellar-mass black holes** are the corpses of the most massive [stars](/article/stars). When such a star exhausts its fuel, its core collapses under its own gravity with nothing left to stop it, crushing several times the mass of the Sun into a region a few kilometres across.

**Supermassive black holes** are the giants — millions to billions of times the Sun's mass — that sit at the centre of almost every large galaxy, including our own. How they grew so big so early in cosmic history is still being worked out.
**Intermediate-mass black holes**, in between the two, are rarer and harder to find, but a growing number of candidates have been spotted.

body.text: At the heart of a black hole, our current theories predict a **singularity** — a point of seemingly infinite density where the known laws of physics break down. What truly happens there is one of the great unsolved problems of physics, awaiting a theory that can unite gravity with quantum mechanics.
Falling In

Suppose you fell toward a black hole feet first. Because gravity grows so much stronger over even a short distance near the horizon, your feet would be pulled far harder than your head. You would be stretched into a long thin stream — a process astronomers cheerfully call **spaghettification**.

To a distant observer watching you fall, something stranger happens: you would appear to slow down and freeze at the event horizon, your image reddening and fading forever, never quite seen to cross. Time itself runs differently in such extreme gravity.

The black hole does leave clues to its presence. Gas spiralling inward forms a flattened, superheated **accretion disk** that glows fiercely across the spectrum, and some black holes fire colossal **jets** of matter out along their poles at nearly the speed of light. We see the black hole not directly, but by the violence it works on everything around it.
Hearing and Seeing the Invisible
For most of history, black holes were known only by inference. Then came two breakthroughs that let us detect them almost directly.

In **2015**, the **LIGO** observatories registered a faint quiver in their instruments — **gravitational waves**, ripples in spacetime set off by two black holes colliding more than a billion light-years away. Einstein had predicted such waves a century earlier and doubted they could ever be measured. We have now heard dozens of these cosmic collisions.

Then, in **2019**, the **Event Horizon Telescope** — a network of radio dishes spanning the globe and acting as one Earth-sized instrument — released the first true image of a black hole: a glowing ring of hot gas encircling the dark silhouette of the giant at the centre of galaxy **M87**. In **2022** the same team imaged **Sagittarius A***, the four-million-solar-mass black hole at the heart of our own Milky Way.

body.title: A black hole in our own galaxy
body.text: The centre of the Milky Way, about 27,000 light-years from Earth, hosts Sagittarius A*. We are in no danger from it — we orbit it at a safe and stately distance, completing one lap roughly every 230 million years.
Not Entirely Black

In the 1970s, **Stephen Hawking** made a startling prediction: black holes are not perfectly black after all. Quantum effects at the very edge of the event horizon should cause them to leak a faint trickle of radiation — now called **Hawking radiation** — and, over unimaginable spans of time, to slowly evaporate.

For a black hole of stellar mass, this process is fantastically slow, taking far longer than the present age of the universe. But it means that even these eternal-seeming objects may have a final chapter, dwindling away in the deep future. That far-off fate, and the role black holes may play in the ultimate destiny of the cosmos, is part of the story explored in [the future of space](/article/the-future-of-space).

Black holes sit at the meeting point of the very large and the very small, of gravity and quantum theory — and so they remain among the most active frontiers in all of science. Each new detection, from a ripple in spacetime to a ring of fire, turns a little more of the unknown into the known.

toc.text: Predicted by Einstein, Found in the Sky

title: Egyptian Astronomy
eyebrow: Reading the calendar of the sky
lede: The Egyptians watched the heavens with practical devotion — timing the life-giving flood by a single star, ordering the night with star-clocks, and giving the world a 365-day year that still ticks beneath our own calendar.

maps.title: The Egyptian night sky
maps.caption: Decans, the circumpolar "imperishable stars", and the band of Sah (Orion) and Sopdet (Sirius).

images.title: The Dendera zodiac
images.caption: A famous Greco-Egyptian sky-map carved in a temple ceiling, now in the Louvre.

images.title: Star-clock ceiling
images.caption: Diagonal tables of decan stars used to tell the hours of the night.

videos.title: The heliacal rising of Sirius
videos.caption: How the dawn return of one star announced the new year and the coming flood.

body.text: A Star That Started the Year

The single most important sight in the Egyptian sky was the **heliacal rising of Sirius** — the morning when the brightest star, called **Sopdet**, first reappeared on the eastern horizon at dawn after a season hidden in the sun's glare. Remarkably, this happened right around the time the **Nile flood** began. The Egyptians tied their new year to that rising, turning a star into the herald of the harvest.
The 365-Day Year

From these observations the Egyptians built a **civil calendar** of 365 days: twelve months of thirty days, plus five extra "birthday of the gods" days at the end. It was one of the first calendars to abandon the awkward moon in favour of a clean solar count — and it is the direct ancestor of the calendar we use today.

body.title: The leap-year problem they spotted
body.text: A true year is about 365¼ days, so the Egyptian calendar slowly drifted against the seasons by one day every four years. Egyptian priests understood this drift; centuries later it was corrected by adding a leap day — the reform behind the Julian and then Gregorian calendars.
Clocks of Star and Shadow
Egyptian astronomy was intensely practical:

items: **Decans** — 36 stars or star-groups that rose in succession through the night, used as a celestial clock to divide darkness into hours.
**Merkhet and bay** — simple sighting instruments (a plumb-line and a slotted palm-rib) for aligning buildings and tracking stars.
**Shadow clocks and water clocks** for measuring the daytime and temple-night hours.

body.text: Aligning Earth to Heaven

This knowledge fed straight into architecture. To set the [Pyramids of Giza](/article/pyramids-of-giza) almost perfectly to true north, surveyors sighted on circumpolar "imperishable stars" — the ones that never set, and so became a natural symbol of eternal life in [Egyptian religion](/article/egyptian-religion).
The same sky that timed the planting of grain also told the dead which way to face for resurrection. For the Egyptians, astronomy, farming, and faith were a single seamless craft.
From these foundations, the long human conversation with the heavens continues throughout the [Space](/category/space) category.

toc.text: A Star That Started the Year

title: Egyptian Religion
lede: A worldview in which the universe ran on cosmic balance, the sun was reborn each dawn, and death was a guarded passage rather than an end — the spiritual machinery that powered three thousand years of Egyptian life.

maps.title: Cult centres of Egypt
maps.caption: Heliopolis, Memphis, Thebes and Abydos — each the home of a great god and theology.

images.title: The weighing of the heart
images.caption: From the Book of the Dead — the heart balanced against the feather of Maat before Osiris.

images.title: Osiris, Isis and Horus
images.caption: The divine family at the centre of Egypt's most important myth of death and rebirth.

videos.title: The journey through the Duat
videos.caption: A reconstruction of the soul's nightly voyage through the underworld with the sun god.

citations.label: The British Museum — Egyptian gods
citations.source: The British Museum, Department of Egypt and Sudan

body.text: Maat: The Order of Things

At the root of Egyptian religion lay a single idea: **maat** — truth, justice, and cosmic balance. Maat was personified as a goddess wearing an ostrich feather, but it was really a principle: the rightness that held the universe together against **isfet**, the ever-pressing force of chaos. Gods upheld maat in the heavens; the pharaoh upheld it on Earth; and ordinary people upheld it by living honestly. Religion was less a matter of belief than of *keeping things in order*.
A Living Pantheon
The Egyptians worshipped a sprawling, flexible family of gods whose roles overlapped and merged over the centuries:

items: **Ra** — the sun, the supreme creative power, sailing his solar barque across the sky.
**Osiris** — murdered, restored, and made lord of the dead, the promise of resurrection.
**Isis** — his devoted wife, mistress of magic and the archetypal mother.
**Horus** — their falcon-headed son, whose living embodiment was the king.
**Anubis** — the jackal who oversaw embalming and guided the dead.
**Thoth** — ibis-headed god of writing, wisdom, and the reckoning of time.

body.text: Gods could fuse (Amun-Ra, Ptah-Sokar-Osiris) as cities and priesthoods rose and fell. This was not contradiction but a theology comfortable with many faces of one truth.

body.title: The first recorded monotheism?
body.text: Around 1350 BCE the pharaoh **Akhenaten** swept the old gods aside in favour of a single deity, the **Aten** (the sun-disk). The experiment collapsed within a generation of his death — but it remains one of history's earliest attempts at an exclusive, single-god religion.
The Afterlife

Egyptian funerary religion was an elaborate technology of continuation. The dead faced judgement in the **Hall of Two Truths**, where Anubis weighed the heart against the feather of Maat while Thoth recorded the verdict. A balanced heart earned eternity in the **Field of Reeds**, an idealised Egypt; a heavy one was devoured by the monster Ammit, and the soul was annihilated.
To make the passage safe, the living equipped their dead with spells — the **Pyramid Texts**, **Coffin Texts**, and later the **Book of the Dead** — alongside the preserved body itself.

That insistence on preserving the body leads directly to the Egyptian art of [mummification](/article/mummification), and the conviction that the dead lived on places the king — both god and man — at the very centre of the cosmos, as we see in the role of [the pharaoh](/article/the-pharaoh).

title: Egyptian Tourism
lede: To visit Egypt is to walk through five thousand years in a few days — from the Giza plateau to the temples of Luxor and the treasures of a new Grand Museum — in one of the oldest and most extraordinary journeys in world travel.

maps.title: The classic Egypt itinerary
maps.caption: Cairo & Giza in the north, Luxor and the Valley of the Kings in the south, linked by the Nile.

images.title: The Grand Egyptian Museum
images.caption: A vast new museum beside the pyramids, home to the complete Tutankhamun collection.

images.title: Temple of Luxor at dusk
images.caption: Floodlit colonnades on the east bank of the Nile in ancient Thebes.

images.title: A felucca on the Nile
images.caption: Traditional sailboats still ply the river between Luxor and Aswan.

videos.title: Egypt in seven days
videos.caption: A traveller's route from the pyramids to the temples of the south.

body.text: A Journey Older Than History

Tourism in Egypt is itself astonishingly old. Greek and Roman travellers were already sightseeing at the pyramids two thousand years ago, scratching graffiti onto monuments that were ancient even then. Today, heritage tourism is one of the pillars of the [modern Egyptian](/article/modern-egypt) economy and one of the great bucket-list journeys on Earth.
The Essential Route
A classic visit traces the Nile from north to south:

items: **Cairo & Giza** — the [Pyramids of Giza](/article/pyramids-of-giza) and the Great Sphinx, plus the new **Grand Egyptian Museum**, the largest archaeological museum in the world, holding the full treasure of Tutankhamun.
**Luxor** — ancient Thebes, with the colossal temples of **Karnak** and **Luxor** on the east bank and the **Valley of the Kings** and **Valley of the Queens** on the west.
**Aswan & Abu Simbel** — the southern frontier, where the rock-cut temples of **Ramesses II** were famously relocated, block by block, to save them from the rising waters of the Aswan dam.
**The Nile cruise** — many travellers sail between Luxor and Aswan, seeing the riverside temples of Edfu and Kom Ombo along the way.

body.title: Saving the monuments
body.text: The 1960s rescue of Abu Simbel — cutting the temples into blocks and rebuilding them on higher ground — sparked the international movement that created the **UNESCO World Heritage** system. Egypt's monuments quite literally helped invent the modern idea of shared human heritage.
Travel as Understanding

Standing before a wall of [hieroglyphs](/article/hieroglyphs) or inside a royal tomb does something no book can: it collapses the distance of millennia. The traveller becomes the final step on the long journey we have followed — from the founding of [ancient Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt), through its gods, kings, and monuments, all the way back to the living, breathing river today.
Every visitor who climbs the Giza plateau joins a line of travellers stretching back, unbroken, for more than two thousand years.

title: Greek Architecture
lede: From the marble temples crowning their hilltops the Greeks worked out a language of columns, proportions, and harmony so satisfying that the world has never stopped speaking it. Banks, parliaments, and museums still wear the face of a Greek temple two and a half thousand years on.

maps.title: The Acropolis of Athens
maps.caption: The sacred rock above the city, crowned by the Parthenon and its companion temples — the showpiece of classical architecture.

images.title: The Parthenon
images.caption: The temple of Athena, its Doric columns subtly curved to correct the eye — perhaps the most influential building ever raised.

images.title: The three orders
images.caption: Doric, Ionic and Corinthian — the column styles whose proportions became the alphabet of Western architecture.

videos.title: How the Greeks built in marble
videos.caption: Quarrying, carving and raising the columns of a temple without mortar or machines.

citations.label: The Met — Greek Architecture
citations.source: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, Heilbrunn Timeline of Art History

body.text: The Temple as a Machine for Beauty

The Greeks did not build the largest structures of the ancient world. Their temples are modest beside the [pyramids of Giza](/article/pyramids-of-giza) or the vaults of Rome. What they built instead was the most *refined* — buildings of such precise proportion and quiet harmony that they have served as the standard of architectural beauty for twenty-five centuries. A Greek temple was, above all, a problem in proportion solved with mathematical care.

The temple itself was conceived as the house of a god, not a hall for worshippers, who gathered outside at an open-air altar. Its essential form was simple: a rectangular room holding the cult statue, set on a stepped platform and ringed by a colonnade of columns supporting a low gabled roof. Everything depended on getting the relationships right — the ratio of a column's height to its width, of the spacing between columns, of the whole to its parts. The Greeks pursued these ratios with the same passion their [philosophers](/article/greek-philosophy) brought to the order of the cosmos.

body.title: Beauty as mathematics
body.text: For the Greeks, beauty was not decoration laid on top of a building but proportion built into its bones. Harmony in stone reflected the same numerical order that **Pythagoras** had found in music and the heavens — architecture as visible mathematics. ## The Three Orders Greek architecture is written in an alphabet of three **orders**, each a complete system of column and the entablature it carries. The **Doric** order, oldest and sternest, has sturdy fluted columns with plain cushion-like tops, rising straight from the platform without a base — the order of the Parthenon, all strength and restraint. The **Ionic** order is lighter and more graceful, its columns slimmer and crowned with the elegant spiral scrolls called volutes. The **Corinthian**, latest and most ornate, tops its columns with baskets of carved acanthus leaves, and would become a favourite of the Romans. These were not just decorative choices but a shared grammar that every Greek builder knew and every later architect would inherit. To name a building "Doric" or "Ionic" is still to describe it in a language the Greeks invented. > Well building hath three conditions: firmness, commodity, and delight. The Roman architect Vitruvius, summing up the Greek inheritance, named the three demands that good building must meet — that it stand firm, serve its purpose, and please the eye. The Greeks satisfied the third with a subtlety that still astonishes. ## The Perfection of the Parthenon The supreme achievement was the **Parthenon**, the temple of Athena raised on the Acropolis of Athens in the 440s and 430s BCE under the statesman Pericles, at the height of Athenian [democracy](/article/greek-democracy). To the casual eye it is a simple rectangle of Doric columns. In truth almost nothing in it is straight. The architects **Iktinos** and **Kallikrates** built in a host of tiny, deliberate distortions to correct the errors of human sight. The platform swells gently upward in the middle, so it will not appear to sag. The columns lean very slightly inward and bulge a little at the centre, so they will not seem to taper or buckle. The corner columns are subtly thicker and more closely spaced. Not a single major line is truly level or vertical, yet the result reads as flawlessly regular — a building bent in secret so that it might appear perfect. It is one of the most sophisticated optical refinements in the history of building. :::note Colour beneath the white We picture Greek temples as gleaming white marble, but that is an accident of time. Originally they blazed with paint — the sculptures and mouldings picked out in red, blue, and gold. The serene whiteness we admire is the ghost of a far more vivid original. ## A Style That Conquered Time Greek architecture did not stay in Greece. The Romans took the orders and married them to their own genius for [engineering](/article/roman-engineering), wrapping Greek columns around their arches, vaults, and domes to build on a scale the Greeks never attempted. Through Rome the classical language passed into the bloodstream of Western building, and across the [ancient architecture](/article/ancient-architecture) of the Mediterranean it became the common tongue of monumental design. It has never gone out of fashion for long. The Renaissance revived it; the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries built courthouses, museums, banks, and capitols in the image of Greek temples to borrow their air of dignity and reason. When a modern democracy wants its parliament to look serious and enduring, it reaches, still, for a row of Greek columns — proof that the grammar worked out on a few Aegean hilltops remains the most quoted in the world.

toc.text: The Temple as a Machine for Beauty

title: Greek Mythology
lede: The Greeks peopled their world with gods who loved, schemed, raged, and blundered exactly as humans do — a glittering, quarrelsome family on Mount Olympus whose stories explained the cosmos, mirrored the human heart, and became the deepest well of imagery in Western art and literature.

maps.title: Mount Olympus
maps.caption: The highest peak in Greece, imagined as the cloud-wrapped home of the twelve Olympian gods.

images.title: Zeus of Artemision
images.caption: A bronze god, arm drawn back to hurl a thunderbolt — the king of Olympus at the height of classical sculpture.

images.title: Athena and the owl
images.caption: The goddess of wisdom and war, patron of Athens, with the owl that became her emblem.

videos.title: The twelve Olympians
videos.caption: Zeus, Hera, Poseidon and the divine family who ruled the Greek cosmos.

citations.label: The Met — Greek Gods and Religious Practices
citations.source: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, Heilbrunn Timeline of Art History

body.text: Gods in Our Own Image

The gods of Greece were not remote, perfect, or unknowable. They were vividly, dangerously human. They fell in love and betrayed one another, nursed grudges, took sides in wars, disguised themselves to meddle in human affairs, and squabbled like an enormous, immortal family. They were stronger and deathless, but not wiser or kinder than the people who worshipped them. In telling stories about such gods, the Greeks were really telling stories about themselves — about jealousy, desire, pride, and fate.

At the head of this divine family stood **Zeus**, lord of the sky and the thunderbolt, who had overthrown his own father to seize power. Around him gathered the twelve **Olympians**: his quarrelsome queen **Hera**; his brother **Poseidon**, who shook the sea and the earth; grey-eyed **Athena**, goddess of wisdom and just war, sprung fully armed from Zeus's own head; the radiant **Apollo** of music, light, and prophecy, and his twin **Artemis** of the hunt; **Aphrodite** of love and desire; **Ares** of brutal war; the cunning messenger **Hermes**; and the rest of that bright, contentious company on Mount Olympus.

body.title: A religion without a holy book
body.text: The Greeks had no Bible, no creed, and no single church. Their "scripture" was poetry — above all the epics of **Homer** and the *Theogony* of **Hesiod**, which gave the gods their genealogy and their tales. Myth was less a set of beliefs to obey than a shared language for thinking about the world. ## From Chaos to Olympus The Greeks had their own account of how everything began. In the beginning, Hesiod sang, there was only **Chaos** — a yawning void — from which emerged **Gaia**, the Earth, and a first generation of vast primal beings. Gaia bore the **Titans**, who ruled before the gods we know. The Titan **Cronus** devoured his own children to keep his throne, until his son Zeus escaped, freed his swallowed siblings, and led the Olympians to victory in a ten-year war against the Titans. So the cosmos the Greeks lived in was the result of a violent struggle between generations — order wrenched out of chaos and held by force. It was a world watched over by gods who had themselves once been rebels, and beneath them by the implacable **Fates**, who measured out every mortal life and to whom even Zeus had to bow. > Count no man happy until he is dead. The grim wisdom of that proverb runs through Greek myth. However glorious a hero, fortune could turn, the gods could be cruel or careless, and a man's destiny was already being spun. The myths are full of mortals — **Oedipus**, **Prometheus**, **Heracles** — who reach too high or are caught in a doom they did not choose, and whose suffering reveals the limits of the human against the divine. ## Heroes and Monsters Between the gods and ordinary mortals stood the **heroes**, half-divine champions whose adventures filled the Greek imagination. **Heracles** performed his twelve impossible labours; **Theseus** threaded the labyrinth to kill the Minotaur; **Perseus** beheaded the snake-haired Medusa; **Jason** sailed for the Golden Fleece; **Odysseus** wandered ten years to come home from the [Trojan War](/article/trojan-war). Their stories were not idle entertainment. They taught courage and its costs, mapped the boundaries of the known world, and gave every Greek city heroes of its own to claim as ancestors. :::note Myth woven into daily life These were not stories the Greeks merely read. Their gods had temples in every city, festivals marked the calendar, oracles like Delphi advised kings, and the [Olympic Games](/article/greek-olympics) themselves were held in honour of Zeus. Myth, [religion](/article/roman-religion), art, and civic life were a single fabric. ## The Stories That Never Died When [Rome](/article/ancient-greece) rose, it took the Greek gods almost wholesale, renaming Zeus as Jupiter and Aphrodite as Venus and carrying their tales into Latin — the foundation of [Roman mythology](/article/roman-mythology). Compared with the solemn divine kingship of [Egyptian religion](/article/egyptian-religion), the Greek gods were startlingly human and approachable, and that very humanity gave their stories a strange endurance. Long after the temples fell silent and no one sacrificed to Zeus, the myths lived on as literature and art. Renaissance painters, Romantic poets, psychologists naming complexes, and modern storytellers have all returned to this inexhaustible well. The Greeks gave the West not a faith that survived but a mythology that did — a cast of gods and heroes through whom we still talk about love, war, pride, and fate.

title: Greek Philosophy
lede: In the cities of ancient Greece a handful of thinkers stopped explaining the world by myth and began explaining it by reason — asking what everything is made of, how we know anything at all, and how a good life should be lived. They invented philosophy, and with it the habit of thought that became science.

maps.title: Ionia, Cradle of Philosophy
maps.caption: The Greek cities of the Asia Minor coast, where the first thinkers began to seek natural rather than divine explanations.

images.title: The School of Athens
images.caption: Raphael's Renaissance fresco imagining Plato and Aristotle at the centre of the whole Greek tradition of thought.

images.title: Bust of Socrates
images.caption: The snub-nosed Athenian who wrote nothing yet changed philosophy forever by asking relentless questions.

videos.title: Socrates, Plato, Aristotle
videos.caption: Three generations of teachers and pupils who set the agenda of Western thought.

body.text: The First Question

Around 600 BCE, in the Greek city of **Miletus** on the coast of Asia Minor, a man named **Thales** asked a question that changed history. Instead of saying that the world was made and ruled by gods, he asked what the world was actually *made of* — and answered, in effect, that everything came from a single underlying substance, which he guessed was water. He was wrong about the answer. But the question was revolutionary, because it sought an explanation in nature itself, by reason, rather than in the will of [the gods](/article/greek-mythology).

These first thinkers, the **Presocratics**, were the world's first scientists as much as its first philosophers. **Anaximander** imagined an infinite, boundless source of all things and even sketched a primitive evolution of life from the sea. **Pythagoras** found that the cosmos was written in numbers and musical ratios. **Heraclitus** declared that everything flows and nothing stays the same, while **Parmenides** argued the opposite, that change is an illusion and reality is one unchanging whole. **Democritus** proposed that all matter is built from tiny indivisible particles he called **atoms** — an astonishing guess, twenty-three centuries before physics confirmed it.

body.title: From myth to logos
body.text: The Greek achievement was the shift from *mythos* to *logos* — from story to reasoned account. The Presocratics insisted that the universe is intelligible, that it follows regular principles a human mind can grasp. That single assumption is the seed of all later [science](/article/the-big-bang). ## Socrates and the Examined Life In fifth-century Athens, philosophy turned from the cosmos to the human soul. **Socrates** wrote nothing and claimed to know nothing, yet he transformed the subject. He wandered the marketplace cornering confident men — politicians, poets, generals — and asking them simple questions: What is justice? What is courage? What is good? Under his relentless questioning their certainties collapsed, revealing that they did not really know what they thought they knew. > The unexamined life is not worth living. That defiant claim, spoken at his trial, became the motto of philosophy itself. Socrates' method of probing question and answer — the **dialectic** — taught that truth is reached not by accepting authority but by testing every belief. It made him beloved by the young and dangerous to the powerful, and in **399 BCE** the Athenian [democracy](/article/greek-democracy) condemned him to death on charges of impiety and corrupting the youth. He drank the hemlock calmly, and his execution turned him into philosophy's first martyr. ## Plato and Aristotle Socrates' greatest pupil, **Plato**, preserved his teacher's voice in brilliant written dialogues and built from it a vast philosophy of his own. Plato argued that the changing things we see are mere shadows of perfect, eternal **Forms** — that somewhere beyond the senses lies the true reality of Justice, Beauty, and the Good, which the mind alone can grasp. In his *Republic* he imagined an ideal state ruled by philosopher-kings, and he founded the **Academy**, the first true university, which endured for centuries. Plato's own greatest pupil, **Aristotle**, then turned the tradition outward again, back toward the physical world. Where Plato looked to an ideal realm, Aristotle observed and classified the one in front of him — dissecting animals, cataloguing constitutions, laying down the rules of **logic** that governed reasoning for two thousand years. He wrote on physics, biology, ethics, politics, poetry, and rhetoric, an encyclopedic mind whose works became the backbone of medieval learning. And, as it happened, he was hired to tutor a Macedonian prince named [Alexander](/article/alexander-the-great). :::note A chain of teachers Socrates taught Plato; Plato taught Aristotle; Aristotle taught Alexander the Great. In four generations the Greek mind passed from the questioning of one barefoot Athenian to the conqueror of the known world — a lineage of teacher and pupil that shaped both thought and empire. ## How to Live After Alexander, philosophy turned increasingly to a single urgent question: how should one live in an uncertain world? The **Stoics** taught that happiness lay in accepting what we cannot control and living according to reason and virtue. The **Epicureans** sought a quiet life of modest pleasure and freedom from fear. The **Cynics** mocked convention and wealth. These schools offered not just theories of the universe but practical philosophies of life, and they spread far beyond Greece. When [Rome](/article/roman-engineering) conquered the Greek world, it absorbed Greek philosophy along with everything else, and Roman thinkers like Cicero, Seneca, and the emperor Marcus Aurelius wrote as devoted students of the Greeks. Through Rome, and later through the Islamic world and the European Renaissance, this inheritance flowed down to us. Nearly every question the West still argues over — about knowledge, justice, the good life, and the nature of reality — was first framed by these Greeks who decided that the world could be understood by thinking.

title: Hieroglyphs
lede: For over three thousand years the Egyptians wrote in a script of birds, eyes, and reeds — a system part picture, part sound — that fell silent for fifteen centuries until a single broken stone gave back its voice.

maps.title: Where the script survives
maps.caption: Temples and tombs from the delta to Nubia preserve millions of carved and painted signs.

images.title: The Rosetta Stone
images.caption: One decree in three scripts — hieroglyphic, demotic and Greek — the key to decipherment.
images.credit: 196 BCE, now in the British Museum

images.title: A cartouche
images.caption: A royal name encircled by a protective oval loop of rope.

videos.title: Cracking the code
videos.caption: How Champollion used the Rosetta Stone to read hieroglyphs again in 1822.

citations.label: The British Museum — the Rosetta Stone

body.text: Pictures That Are Also Sounds
The word *hieroglyph* is Greek for "sacred carving." The script appeared around **3100 BCE** and combines three kinds of sign:

items: **Logograms** — a picture standing for a whole word (a sun-disk for "sun").
**Phonograms** — signs standing for sounds, from single consonants to groups of two or three.
**Determinatives** — silent signs added to the end of a word to clarify its meaning.

body.text: This blend made the system rich but demanding, and literacy was the prized preserve of trained **scribes**, who enjoyed status and exemption from hard labour. For everyday writing, Egyptians used faster cursive forms — **hieratic**, and later **demotic** — brushed in ink on papyrus.
Writing for Eternity

Monumental hieroglyphs were never merely informative; they were **magical**. To write a thing was, in a sense, to make it real and lasting, which is why temple and tomb walls are covered in carved text meant to secure offerings, protection, and eternal life. Royal names were wrapped in a protective oval, the **cartouche**.

body.title: The silence and the key
body.text: After the temples closed in the early centuries CE, the knowledge of how to read hieroglyphs died out completely for some **1,400 years**. The script became beautiful, mysterious decoration — until the **Rosetta Stone**, carved with the same decree in hieroglyphic, demotic, and Greek, let **Jean-François Champollion** crack the code in 1822.
A Civilization Speaks Again

Decipherment turned silent walls back into a library, founding the science of **Egyptology** and opening the literature, religion, and history of [ancient Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt) to the modern world. The link between sacred writing and sacred belief runs deep — explored further in [Egyptian religion](/article/egyptian-religion) and across the [Sacred Books](/category/sacred-books) category.
A script designed to defeat death did exactly that: the Egyptians wrote so that they would be remembered, and we remember them.

title: Julius Caesar
eyebrow: The man who broke the Republic
lede: Soldier, statesman, and writer of genius, Julius Caesar conquered Gaul, crossed the Rubicon into civil war, loved a queen of Egypt, and made himself master of Rome — only to fall beneath the daggers of senators who killed him to save a Republic that died with him anyway.

maps.title: The Conquest of Gaul
maps.caption: Caesar's eight-year campaign (58–50 BCE) brought all of modern France and Belgium under Roman rule and made him the richest man in the state.

maps.title: The Civil War
maps.caption: From the Rubicon to Pharsalus to Egypt — the campaigns by which Caesar defeated Pompey and the Senate.

images.title: The Tusculum portrait
images.caption: One of the few likenesses of Caesar carved within living memory of the man himself.

images.title: Caesar and Cleopatra
images.caption: The Roman general and the last pharaoh of Egypt — a political alliance that became a legend.

videos.title: Crossing the Rubicon
videos.caption: The day a general led his army against his own city and changed the course of Roman history.

citations.label: Julius Caesar — Commentarii de Bello Gallico
citations.source: Caesar's own account of the Gallic War (Loeb Classical Library)

citations.label: Suetonius — The Life of the Deified Julius
citations.source: Suetonius, The Twelve Caesars (Loeb Classical Library)

body.text: A Patrician on the Rise

Gaius Julius Caesar was born in **100 BCE** into an old but faded patrician family that claimed descent from the goddess **Venus** herself — a pedigree he would later turn to political advantage. He came of age in the violent, factional Rome of [the late Republic](/article/roman-republic), where the murders of Marius and Sulla had shown that armies, not arguments, now decided who ruled. A gifted speaker and a shameless borrower of money, Caesar climbed the ladder of offices by charm, bribery, and an unfailing instinct for where power was moving.

His decisive move was an alliance. In **60 BCE** he joined with Rome's richest man, **Crassus**, and its greatest general, **Pompey**, in the informal pact historians call the First Triumvirate. With their backing he won the consulship — and then the prize that would make him: a long military command in Gaul.
The Conquest of Gaul

For eight years Caesar waged war across the lands of modern France and Belgium, and even raided Britain and crossed the Rhine into Germany. By the campaign's end he had defeated hundreds of tribes, killed or enslaved enormous numbers, and added a vast new province to the empire. He recorded it all himself in the *Commentaries on the Gallic War*, a masterpiece of clear Latin prose written, tellingly, in the third person — propaganda so well crafted that it is still read as literature two thousand years later.

body.title: Caesar wrote his own legend
body.text: By publishing dispatches in plain, vivid Latin, Caesar made himself the hero of his own story in the minds of ordinary Romans. He understood, as few generals have, that the account of a victory can matter as much as the victory itself. Gaul made Caesar fabulously rich and gave him a battle-hardened [army](/article/roman-army) devoted to him personally. To the frightened Senate, that combination looked exactly like the tyranny the Republic had been built to prevent. ## Crossing the Rubicon When the Senate, prodded by Pompey, ordered Caesar to disband his army and return to Rome as a private citizen — where his enemies waited to destroy him — he chose war instead. In **January 49 BCE** he led a single legion across the **Rubicon**, the small river marking the boundary a general was forbidden to cross under arms. > The die is cast. With those words, tradition says, he plunged Rome into civil war. The campaign that followed carried him the length of the Mediterranean. He broke Pompey's larger army at **Pharsalus** in Greece, and when Pompey fled to [Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt), Caesar pursued. ## Egypt and Cleopatra In Alexandria, Caesar was drawn into a dynastic quarrel of the Ptolemies and into the arms of **Cleopatra VII**, the brilliant young queen who would become the last [pharaoh](/article/the-pharaoh) of Egypt. He restored her to a throne she shared, fathered a son she named Caesarion, and lingered in the richest kingdom of the ancient world. The meeting of the Roman master of the west and the Greek queen of the Nile became one of history's enduring legends — and bound Rome's fate ever closer to Egypt's grain and gold. ## The Ides of March Returning to Rome supreme, Caesar gathered every honour into himself: **dictator for life**, his image on the coinage, a month renamed *July* in his honour. He reformed the calendar, relieved debt, and planned grand works — but he also wore the trappings of a king in a city that still swore its old oath against them. To a band of senators led by **Brutus** and **Cassius**, this was tyranny that had to be cut out. On **15 March 44 BCE** — the Ides of March — they surrounded him in the Senate and stabbed him twenty-three times at the foot of Pompey's statue. :::note The murder that backfired The assassins killed Caesar to save the Republic, but they had no plan for what came next. Their act unleashed another round of civil war, at the end of which Caesar's heir Octavian — soon to be **Augustus** — gathered all power into one pair of hands and founded the [Roman Empire](/article/roman-empire). In death Caesar achieved what the conspirators most feared: he became a god, and his name became the very word for emperor — *Kaiser*, *Tsar*, *Caesar*.

title: Mars
lede: Half the size of Earth, painted rust-red by iron in its soil, and wrapped in a thin cold air, Mars is the world that has gripped human imagination like no other. Once warm and wet, today a frozen desert, it is the most explored planet beyond our own — and the leading candidate for humanity's first steps onto another world.

maps.title: The face of Mars
maps.caption: Olympus Mons, the vast Tharsis volcanoes, the Valles Marineris canyon system, and the polar ice caps.

images.title: The rusty surface
images.caption: A cold, dry, iron-stained desert under a pink-tinged sky — photographed from the ground by NASA's rovers.

images.title: Ancient riverbeds
images.caption: Branching channels and layered sediments — evidence that liquid water once flowed across the Martian surface.
images.credit: NASA / JPL / University of Arizona, HiRISE

images.title: Ingenuity in flight
images.caption: The first powered, controlled flight on another planet — a small helicopter that flew in Mars's thin air in 2021.

videos.title: A world that lost its water
videos.caption: How Mars went from a warmer, wetter past to the frozen desert we see today.

videos.title: Driving on Mars
videos.caption: How robotic rovers explore the surface, drill rock, and search for signs of ancient life.

body.text: The Red Planet

Mars glows a distinctive rusty red in the night sky, and the reason is literally rust: its surface is rich in **iron oxide**, the same compound that reddens corroded metal on Earth. The ancients named it after gods of war for that blood-coloured light — the Romans called it Mars, and we still do.

It is a smaller, colder, drier sibling of Earth. Mars is about half our planet's diameter and has roughly a third of its gravity, so a person who weighs 60 kilograms on Earth would weigh barely 23 on Mars. Its thin atmosphere is almost entirely carbon dioxide and exerts less than one percent of Earth's surface pressure — far too little to breathe, and too thin to hold much warmth. Surface temperatures average a frigid −60°C.

body.title: A day that feels like home
body.text: Despite all its differences, Mars keeps a familiar rhythm. A Martian day — called a **sol** — lasts 24 hours and 39 minutes, remarkably close to Earth's. Its axis is tilted much like ours, too, so Mars has seasons. These similarities are part of why it feels, of all the planets, the most like a place humans could one day live.
A Landscape of Extremes

For a small world, Mars has the most dramatic terrain in the Solar System. It is home to **Olympus Mons**, the largest known volcano anywhere — a gently sloping mountain about 22 kilometres high, nearly three times the height of Everest, and so broad it would cover much of a country. Stretching across the equator is **Valles Marineris**, a system of canyons over 4,000 kilometres long and in places several kilometres deep, dwarfing the Grand Canyon.

At its poles lie shining **ice caps** of frozen water and carbon dioxide that grow and shrink with the seasons. Vast dust storms occasionally swell to engulf the entire planet, and fine red dust coats everything — a constant hazard for the robots, and one day the people, who explore it.
The Ghost of Water

Mars today is bone dry at the surface, but it was not always so. Orbiters and rovers have found unmistakable signatures of a watery past: dried-up **river valleys**, deltas where rivers once emptied into lakes, rounded pebbles shaped by flowing water, and minerals that only form in its presence. Billions of years ago, Mars appears to have been warmer and wetter, with rivers, lakes, perhaps even a shallow ocean.

Where there was water, there may have been life. The search for evidence of ancient Martian microbes — long extinct or perhaps still clinging on underground — is the single greatest driver of Mars exploration.

So what happened? Mars lost its protective magnetic field early in its history, and without that shield the solar wind gradually stripped away most of its atmosphere. As the air thinned, the planet grew cold, its water froze or escaped to space, and the world we now see was left behind. Much of its remaining water survives as ice, locked in the poles and buried beneath the surface.
Two Little Moons

Mars has two small, lumpy moons, **Phobos** and **Deimos** — names meaning "fear" and "dread," the companions of the war god. They are most likely captured asteroids, just a few kilometres across. Phobos orbits so close and so fast that it crosses the Martian sky twice a day and is slowly spiralling inward; in tens of millions of years it will break apart, perhaps giving Mars a temporary ring.
A Planet Full of Robots
No world beyond Earth has been studied as intensely as Mars. A fleet of orbiters maps it from above, while a lineage of **rovers** has trundled across its surface, growing ever more capable:

items: **Sojourner** (1997) — the first wheels on Mars, a microwave-sized proof of concept.
**Spirit and Opportunity** (2004) — twin geologists that found firm evidence of past water; Opportunity worked for nearly 15 years.
**Curiosity** (2012) — a car-sized nuclear-powered laboratory that confirmed Mars once had habitable conditions.
**Perseverance** (2021) — now caching rock samples for a future return to Earth, and carrying **Ingenuity**, the first aircraft to fly on another planet.

body.text: These missions, and the engineering feats behind landing on a distant world, are explored further in [space missions](/article/space-missions).
The Next Giant Leap

Of all the planets, Mars is the one humans seriously plan to visit. It is reachable, it has water ice and raw materials, and its day-length and seasons make it comparatively hospitable — by the brutal standards of space. Both national space agencies and private companies are designing the rockets, habitats, and life-support systems that a crewed mission would require.

The obstacles are immense: a journey of six to nine months each way, exposure to space radiation, the challenge of living off the land, and the sheer cost. But Mars represents the most concrete answer to an old question — whether humanity will become a species that lives on more than one world. That ambition, and what a future on the red planet might look like, runs through [the future of space](/article/the-future-of-space).

title: Modern Egypt
eyebrow: From the pharaohs to the present
lede: The land of the pharaohs is also a vibrant modern nation of more than a hundred million people — shaped by Islam and Arabic, by the Suez Canal and the Aswan High Dam, and by an unbroken sense of one of the world's oldest identities.

maps.title: The Arab Republic of Egypt
maps.caption: Cairo, Alexandria, the Nile valley and delta, the Suez Canal and the Sinai Peninsula.

images.title: Cairo on the Nile
images.caption: The largest city in the Arab world, where medieval minarets meet a modern megacity.

images.title: The Suez Canal
images.caption: Opened 1869, the artificial waterway linking the Mediterranean and the Red Sea.

videos.title: A nation on one river
videos.caption: How 100 million people still live clustered along the same green Nile valley.

citations.label: The British Museum — Egypt after the pharaohs

body.text: The Long Aftermath

When Cleopatra VII died in 30 BCE, Egypt became a province of **Rome**, then of **Byzantium**. In 641 CE the **Arab conquest** brought Islam and the Arabic language, which gradually became the country's faith and tongue. Over the centuries Egypt was ruled from afar and up close — by the Fatimids and Mamluks, by the Ottomans, and after Napoleon's brief expedition, increasingly under European influence.
Building the Modern Nation
Two great engineering projects reshaped the country:

items: The **Suez Canal** (opened 1869) cut a sea route between the Mediterranean and the Red Sea, making Egypt a strategic crossroads of world trade — and a target of imperial rivalry.

The **Aswan High Dam** (completed 1970) finally tamed the annual Nile flood, generating power and securing water, but ending the natural cycle of silt that had fed Egyptian farming for five thousand years.

body.text: Egypt won full independence over the first half of the twentieth century, becoming a **republic in 1953**. Today it is the most populous Arab country and a cultural powerhouse of film, music, and scholarship.

body.title: An identity that never broke
body.text: Few modern nations sit so directly on top of their ancient selves. Egyptians today live on the same river, beneath the same monuments, carrying a continuous sense of "Egypt" that stretches back five millennia.
The Past as a Living Resource

The legacy of [the pharaohs](/article/the-pharaoh) is not only a matter of pride; it is a pillar of the economy. The monuments of [ancient Egypt](/article/ancient-egypt) draw millions of visitors each year, making heritage one of the nation's great modern industries — the story we follow next, into [Egyptian tourism](/article/egypt-tourism).

toc.text: The Past as a Living Resource

title: Mummification
lede: The Egyptian art of preserving the dead was not morbid but hopeful — a meticulous seventy-day craft of chemistry, surgery, and ritual designed to keep the body intact so the soul could live forever.

maps.title: Cities of the dead
maps.caption: Vast necropolises at Saqqara, Giza and Thebes housed the embalmed across millennia.

images.title: Canopic jars
images.caption: Four vessels guarded by the Sons of Horus, holding the preserved internal organs.

images.title: A wrapped mummy
images.caption: Layers of resin-soaked linen, amulets, and a portrait mask over the embalmed body.

videos.title: The seventy-day rite
videos.caption: From natron drying to the final wrapping and the Opening of the Mouth ceremony.

body.text: Why Preserve the Body?

To the Egyptians, a person was made of several parts — including the **ka** (life-force) and **ba** (personality) — that needed a recognisable, intact **body** to return to. If the body decayed, the soul had no home, and eternal life was lost. Mummification, then, was the indispensable technology of the [Egyptian afterlife](/article/egyptian-religion): the careful preservation of the dead so that they might live again.
The Seventy-Day Craft
The full process, reserved for those who could afford it, took about **seventy days** and followed a precise sequence:

items: **Removal of organs** — the lungs, liver, stomach, and intestines were taken out and preserved separately in **canopic jars**; the brain, thought useless, was discarded.
**The heart stayed** — left in place because it was believed to be the seat of thought and would be weighed in judgement.
**Drying** — the body was packed in and covered with **natron**, a natural drying salt, for about forty days until all moisture was gone.
**Wrapping** — the desiccated body was anointed with oils and resins and bound in hundreds of metres of linen, with protective **amulets** tucked between the layers.
**The mask and rites** — a portrait mask was placed over the face, and the **Opening of the Mouth** ceremony ritually restored the senses for the next life.

body.title: Accidental science
body.text: Egyptian embalmers accumulated real anatomical knowledge through this work, and the surviving medical papyri show a tradition of careful, empirical observation. In preserving the dead, they advanced the understanding of the living body — a thread that runs into the history of [medicine](/category/medicine).
Bodies That Outlived Empires
